title: Deploying to the Internet
subtitle: Make your project live for the whole world to see

sections.content:
Everything you've built so far runs on **localhost** — your own computer. Nobody else can see it. That changes now.

Deployment means putting your application on a server that's connected to the internet 24/7, so anyone with the URL can use it. It sounds complicated, but modern platforms have made it remarkably simple. You push your code, they handle the rest.

We'll use **Render** because it has a generous free tier, works directly with GitHub, and requires zero server configuration. Your app will be live in minutes.
sections.analogy: localhost is like cooking a meal in your kitchen — only people in your house can eat it. Deploying is like opening a restaurant: anyone can walk in from anywhere. Render is like a restaurant space that's already set up with a kitchen, tables, and an address — you just bring the food (your code).

sections.instruction: Deployment steps with Render (free tier). Follow these in order and you'll have a live URL in about 10 minutes.

steps.title: Push to GitHub
steps.description: Make sure your project is committed and pushed to a GitHub repository. Render will pull your code directly from GitHub, so everything needs to be up to date.

steps.title: Sign up at render.com
steps.description: Use 'Sign up with GitHub' for the easiest setup. This lets Render access your repositories without extra configuration.

steps.title: Create New Web Service
steps.description: Click 'New' → 'Web Service' and connect your GitHub repository. Render will ask which repo to deploy.

steps.title: Configure settings
steps.description: Build command: npm install. Start command: node app.js. These tell Render how to set up and run your app. The build command installs dependencies, and the start command launches your server.

steps.title: Add environment variables
steps.description: Copy your .env values into Render's environment settings. Never commit them to git — enter them directly in the Render dashboard. Each key-value pair goes in separately.

steps.title: Deploy!
steps.description: Click Deploy and wait for the build. You'll see logs streaming in real time. When the status turns green, your app is live at a .onrender.com URL that you can share with anyone.

sections.instruction: Deploy your project by working through this checklist. Take your time — the first deployment always takes a few tries, and that's completely normal.
checklistItems: Deployment succeeded — I can visit my public URL

sections.question: What does a deployment platform like Render do when you push code to GitHub?
options: It emails you a zip file of your project
It automatically pulls your code, builds it, and runs it on their servers
It creates a new GitHub repository
It downloads your code to your local machine
sections.explanation: Render connects to your GitHub repo and automatically deploys when you push — pull, build, run, all handled for you.

sections.prompt: When deploying a Node.js app, the platform needs to know the _____ command to start your server (e.g., 'node app.js').
sections.hint: It's the command that launches your application — what you'd type in the terminal to run it.
sections.successMessage: Correct — the start command tells the deployment platform how to launch your application.

sections.content:
Take a moment to appreciate what just happened. Someone on the other side of the world can now type your URL into their browser and use something **you built**. You wrote the code, you deployed it, and it's running on a real server right now.

This is the moment most developers remember forever — the first time their project went live.
sections.tip: Bookmark your deployment URL and share it with someone. Showing off what you've built is one of the best motivators to keep learning.

completionMessage: Your project is live on the internet. You've gone from writing code on your own machine to deploying a real web service that anyone can access. That's a massive milestone.
